driver.body: Driver 3 is the one driver that **literally moves T in the moat identity M = D × B × L × T** — trust is not an atmosphere here, it is a term with a term-owner. The logic is a ledger of leaks: fraud losses and compliance failures are trust leaking out, and every prevented fraud and every clean audit is trust retained and then **re-lent to all six domains** [S-058]. That re-lending is what makes T a shared asset rather than a cost line inside Payments alone. Crucially, Driver 3 is defensive by construction — it takes the credit exposure opened by Driver 2 and the payment flows opened by Driver 1 and defends both, and it does so **using signals it does not itself generate** [S-058]. That last clause is the whole flywheel in miniature: the term-owner for trust survives on cross-fed data, so lifting T is impossible for any single-vertical competitor that touches only one surface.

mechanisms.body:
The fraud engine scores **every transaction inside the ~40ms RISK node** carried over from Driver 1's payment architecture — real-time, in the authorization path, not a nightly batch [S-059]. What it scores on is the point: its features are the **third and fourth cross-domain couplings** of the payments domain. Fraud signals arrive from D6, the data platform, but D6 does not conjure them — it assembles them from two upstream surfaces no payments competitor can reach:

- **D1 location telemetry** — is this ride's device physically where the card says it is? [S-059]
- **D5 device fingerprints** — is this the same hardware that onboarded the user? [S-059]

So a card-not-present transaction that geolocates **300km from the user's D1 device** is scored differently from one that sits inside their normal mobility envelope [S-059]. The payoff is the rare two-sided win: cross-domain fraud features cut fraud from **18 to 7 basis points of TPV while lowering the false-positive decline rate 22%** — trust up on both sides of the ledger, fewer good customers wrongly blocked and fewer bad ones getting through [S-409].

The configuration encodes the thesis in two design choices [S-410]. First, the **default response to elevated risk is step-up, not decline** — preserve the good customer, protect trust on the acceptance side. Second, the **trusted_corridor override spends earned trust to remove friction**, the direct mechanical expression of T as a compounding asset: past clean behavior buys future frictionlessness. And the **courier_collusion rule is pure cross-domain** — it can exist only because D3 payouts and D4 funding sources live in one ledger, so a courier paying themselves through a fraudulent order is visible where a siloed competitor sees two unrelated events [S-410].

Trust is also **regulatory**, and this mechanism owns that face of T. Kepler holds or passports payment/e-money and lending licences across **14 countries**, and Driver 3 owns the reconciliation and reporting node from the payment architecture [S-060]. The mechanism chains directly out of 3.1: **the same identity graph that powers fraud scoring powers KYC/KYB and sanctions screening**, so compliance is a reuse of the trust infrastructure, not a parallel cost centre standing beside it [S-060]. The economics of that reuse are measurable — consolidating KYC across domains, so **one verified identity is reused by D1, D3, D4, and D5, cut per-user onboarding cost 38%** [S-411]. But the cost saving is the smaller half. The larger half is that the identity itself becomes a moat asset: a **once-verified Kepler user onboards into every new domain with zero incremental friction** [S-411]. That is the sentence that turns compliance from a tax into a distribution engine — **trust as distribution**, where each domain a user is verified for lowers the activation energy for the next, compounding T across the whole flywheel rather than spending it once.
